revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Shinto shrine]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24744680692|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[ja:比奈麻治比売命神社]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Hinamajihimemikoto Shrine | native_name = 比奈麻治比売命神社 | image = Hinamadihime torii.jpg | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Hinamajihimemikoto Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|36.118174|133.072166}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }} {{nihongo|'''Hinamajihimemikoto Shrine'''|比奈麻治比売命神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Oki Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|en|Shikinaisha|zh|式內社|qid=Q134917286|1=Shikinaisha}} * {{ill|Shikinai Shosha|lt=Shikinai Shōsha|en|Shikinai Shosha|ja|式内小社|simple|Shikinai Shosha|qid=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} * {{ill|Q135160342|ltq=Q135160342|qid=Q135160342|1=Kokuhei-sha}} * {{ill|Q135026601|ltq=Q135026601|qid=Q135026601|1=Shikinai Hiteisha}} == part of (P361) == * {{ill|Q11659043|ltq=Q11659043|qid=Q11659043|1=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province}} ** Series ordinal: 4 ** P155: {{ill|Kai Shrine|lt=Kai Shrine|ja|海神社 (西ノ島町)|qid=Q11559097|1=Kai Shrine}} ** P156: {{ill|Q135041140|ltq=Q135041140|qid=Q135041140|1=Make-no-Mikoto Shrine}} == located in the administrative territorial entity (P131) == * {{ill|Q543193|ltq=Q543193|qid=Q543193|1=Nishinoshima, Shimane}} * {{ill|Oki Province|lt=Oki Province|cs|Provincie Oki|de|Provinz Oki|en|Oki Province|es|Provincia de Oki|fa|ولایت اوکی|fr|Province d'Oki|id|Provinsi Oki|it|Oki (provincia)|ja|隠岐国|ko|오키국|lt|Okio žemė|nl|Oki (provincie)|pt|Província de Oki|ru|Оки (провинция)|simple|Oki Province|sk|Oki (provincia)|sr|Провинција Оки|tr|Oki (vilayet)|uk|Провінція Окі|zh_min_nan|Oki-kok|zh|隱岐國|qid=Q1063829|1=Oki Province}} * {{ill|Q11584565|ltq=Q11584565|qid=Q11584565|1=Chibu district}} == coordinate location (P625) == * {{coord|36.118174|133.072166}} == image (P18) == * Hinamadihime torii.jpg == OpenStreetMap way ID (P10689) == * 1379008223 == Kokugakuin University Digital Museum entry ID (P13677) == * 182995 ** P3831: {{ill|Q135159299|ltq=Q135159299|qid=Q135159299|1=Kokugakuin University Shrine database}} == modern shrine ranking (P13723) == * {{ill|Q134917284|ltq=Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} ** End time: {'time': '+1946-02-02T00:00:00Z', 'timezone': 0, 'before': 0, 'after': 0, 'precision': 11, 'calendarmodel': 'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q1985727'} ** P1534: {{ill|Shinto Directive|lt=Shinto Directive|en|Shinto Directive|fa|بخشنامه شینتو|fr|Directive shinto|id|Pengarahan Shinto|ja|神道指令|zh|神道指令|qid=Q3029647|1=Shinto Directive}} == P14005 (P14005) == * {{ill|Q11504610|ltq=Q11504610|qid=Q11504610|1=Unranked}} * {{ill|Q11071125|ltq=Q11071125|qid=Q11071125|1=Junior Fifth Rank}}Kokugakuin University Shrine database * {{ill|Q11123280|ltq=Q11123280|qid=Q11123280|1=Senior Fifth Rank}}Kokugakuin University Shrine database * {{ill|Q11545372|ltq=Q11545372|qid=Q11545372|1=Senior Sixth Rank}}Kokugakuin University Shrine database * {{ill|Q11071127|ltq=Q11071127|qid=Q11071127|1=Junior Fourth Rank}}Kokugakuin University Shrine database == country (P17) == * {{ill|Q17|ltq=Q17|qid=Q17|1=Japan}} == name in kana (P1814) == * ひなまじひめみことじんじゃ == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '島根県隠岐郡西ノ島町宇賀1685', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:比奈麻治比売命神社]] {{wikidata link|Q133940165}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Hinamadihime-no-Mikoto Shrine |Image = [[File:hinamadihime_torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|qid=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|qid=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}{{ill|lt=Nishinoshima Town|Q543193|qid=Q543193|1=Nishinoshima, Shimane}} Uga 1685{{cite web|url=https://www.shimane-jinjacho.or.jp/nishinoshima/b29be45bfd35007246dcdf1e627ca4a7601874ce.html|title=Hinamadihime-no-Mikoto Shrine - Shrine Details│Shimane Prefecture Shrine Association|accessdate=2025-04-12}} |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity = Hinamadihime-no-Mikoto{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=477}} |Sacred object = Wooden statue or black stone{{sfn|Kamada|1960|p=69}} |Shrine rank = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|Q134917287|qid=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} (minor shrine), former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=477}} |Founded = |Main hall = Kasuga-zukuri with corner rafters{{sfn|Makabe & Kuroda|2016|p=544}} |Alternative names = Sumi Daimyōjin{{sfn|Kamada|1960|p=67}} |Pilgrimage affiliation = |Annual festival = July 28{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=311}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Hinamadihime
no-Mikoto Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Hinamadihime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (ひなまじひめみことじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Nishinoshima Town|Q543193|qid=Q543193|1=Nishinoshima, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|qid=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|qid=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} (minor shrine), and its former shrine rank was village shrine. It is commonly known as '''Sumi (Sun) Daimyōjin'''. === Overview === Originally, Hinamadihime-no-Mikoto Shrine was located on a mountain above “Sumi-no-ura,” at the northeastern tip of {{ill|lt=Nishinoshima|Q5004035|qid=Q5004035|1=Nishinoshima (Shimane)}}{{sfn|Kamada|1960|p=67}}. It is an ancient shrine whose name appears in the {{ill|lt=Six National Histories|Q1151168|qid=Q1151168|1=Rikkokushi}} from as early as the 8th century, and the divine power of its enshrined deity, '''Hinamadihime-no-Mikoto''', is thought to be related to {{ill|lt=fire|Q3196|qid=Q3196|1=Fire}}{{sfn|Rika-no-Ya|1987|pp=232–233}}. It is also listed in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}', but after the medieval period it ceased to appear in historical records{{sfn|Matsuura|1983|p=944}}. In the early modern period, it reappears in documents under names such as “Sumi Daimyōjin” as the {{ill|lt=Ubusuna deity|Q1392097|qid=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} of the Uga area. Because its location was inconvenient for worship as an ubusuna shrine, it was ultimately relocated in Shōwa 3 (1928) to its present site closer to the settlement{{sfn|Kamada|1960|p=67}}. === Enshrined deity === {{see also|#Legends}} ; Hinamadihime-no-Mikoto From the miraculous accounts described below, the deity’s divine power is thought to be connected with fire, and the name “Hinamachi” is interpreted as “true spirit of fire”{{sfn|Rika-no-Ya|1987|pp=232–233}}{{sfn|Matsuura|1987|p=37}}. From the medieval period onward, tales of the sacred fire came to be confused with episodes of {{ill|lt=Yakihi Shrine|Q11567668|qid=Q11567668|1=Takuhi Shrine}}{{sfn|Rika-no-Ya|1987|p=233}}. She is sometimes regarded as another name for {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Iku-tamayori-hime|1=UNKNOWN}}, but this is thought to reflect the intentions of the shrine family{{sfn|Matsuura|1983|p=944}}. Concerning the sacred object, a shrine inspection diary from Meiji 2 records “the sacred body is a wooden statue,” while an old survey ledger states “a black stone (omitted)… might this truly be the sacred body”{{sfn|Kamada|1960|p=69}}. Within Shimane Prefecture, she is enshrined only at Hinamadihime-no-Mikoto Shrine{{sfn|Rika-no-Ya|1987|p=233}}, but within the grounds of Kashima Shrine in {{ill|lt=Izumo City|Q825166|qid=Q825166|1=Izumo, Shimane}}, the subsidiary Himori Shrine enshrines “Hinamochi-hime-no-Mikoto”{{sfn|Ōgi-no-Ya|1987|p=228}}. Outside Shimane, she is enshrined at a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|lt=Kushida Shrine (Fukuoka City)|Q865839|qid=Q865839|lt=Kushida Shrine|1=Kushida Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Fukuoka City|Q26600|qid=Q26600|1=Fukuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|qid=Q123258|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}{{sfn|Mihara, ed.|1980|p=200}}, and at Yakihi Shrine in {{ill|lt=Saikai City|Q520267|qid=Q520267|1=Saikai, Nagasaki}}, {{ill|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture|Q169376|qid=Q169376|1=Nagasaki Prefecture}}{{cite web|url=https://www.nagasaki-jinjacho.or.jp/search-seihi.html|title=Nagasaki Prefecture Shrine Association│Shrine Search|accessdate=2025-04-12}}. Formerly, at {{ill|lt=Himine Shrine (Kitakyushu City)|UNKNOWN|lt=Himine Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Kitakyushu City|Q188806|qid=Q188806|1=Kitakyushu}}, Fukuoka Prefecture, a tradition stated: “The deity Hinamachihime of Oki Province manifested on this mountain; therefore a shrine was established and worshiped,” and she was enshrined as the principal deity{{sfn|Onga District Board of Education, ed.|1986|pp=1145–1147}}. === Facilities === === Present site === [[File:Hinamadihime honden.jpg|thumb|300px|Main hall of Hinamadihime-no-Mikoto Shrine]] ; Shrine buildings * Main hall – A modified {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|qid=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}} style with a frontage of 1.5 ken and a depth of 1.5 ken{{sfn|Matsuura|1983|p=945}} (Kasuga-zukuri with corner rafters{{sfn|Makabe & Kuroda|2016|p=544}}). * Worship hall – Frontage 4 ken, depth 4 ken{{sfn|Matsuura|1983|p=945}}. ; Subsidiary shrines Misaki Shrine, Tenman Shrine, and Hachidai Ryūō Shrine were merged into this shrine in Meiji 40 (1907){{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=312}}{{efn|There are also accounts stating that Tenman Shrine was merged in Meiji 13 (1880) or at the end of the Taishō period, and Misaki Shrine at the end of the Taishō period{{sfn|Kamada|1960|p=68}}{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=314}}.}}. At the time of the relocation in Shōwa 3 (1928), they were left at the former site, but in Shōwa 45 (1970) they were moved to the precincts of the present site{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=312}}. * Misaki Shrine – A wooden shrine dedicated to a water deity{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=311}}. Formerly located near the residence of the Yokoya (priest, the Uno family){{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=314}}. * Tenman Shrine – A wooden shrine dedicated to Tenjin{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=311}}. Formerly located in a place called “Tenjin Yashiki”{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=314}}. A {{ill|lt=banner|Q1068772|qid=Q1068772|1=Nobori}} bearing the ink inscription “Namu Myōhō Renge Kyō” is offered{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=311}}. * Hachidai Ryūō Shrine – A stone monument inscribed “White Dragon King Deity”{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=312}}. The former site is unknown{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=314}}. ; Shrine office Uses a remodeled lodging hut{{efn|After praying to the ubusuna shrine for recovery from illness, safe childbirth, abundant catches, school admission, etc., there was a custom called “okomori,” in which one would spend the night in a hut within the shrine precincts called a “komosha” or “komoriya” as thanks for the fulfillment of the prayer{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=310}}.}}{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=311}}. === Former site === Located on a mountain above “Sumi-no-ura”{{efn|Also written “Sun”{{sfn|Matsuura|1983|p=943}}.}} at the northeastern tip of Nishinoshima{{sfn|Matsuura|1983|p=943}}. A stone monument marking the former site was erected in Shōwa 45 (1970){{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=311}}. * Main hall – A modified Kasuga-zukuri with a frontage of 1 ken and a depth of 1.5 ken{{sfn|Matsuura|1983|p=945}}. Beneath the main hall, a stone is buried, with its upper part exposed above ground{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=311}}. === History === According to an entry from Enryaku 18 (799) in the '{{ill|lt=Nihon Kōki|Q1077029|qid=Q1077029|1=Nihon Kōki}}', {{ill|lt=Kura no Sukune Kamo-maro|Q45828962|qid=Q45828962|lt=Kura no Sukune Kamo-maro|1=Kura no Sukune Kamo-maro}}, a member of a {{ill|lt=Japanese envoy to Balhae|Q11642473|qid=Q11642473|1=Japanese missions to Balhae}} who had drifted at sea, relied on a firelight miracle granted by Hinamadihime-no-Mikoto to return safely, leading to the shrine being elevated to an officially supported shrine{{sfn|Kamada|1960|p=69}}. The place where Kura no Sukune is said to have landed is called “Kuranotani”{{efn|There is also a tradition identifying a place called “Kurashiki” as where he landed and rested{{sfn|Kamada|1960|p=69}}.}}, and this area falls within the parish of Hinamadihime-no-Mikoto Shrine{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=311}}. In Shōwa 5 (838), the shrine’s deity was promoted from no rank to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade ({{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|qid=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}); in Jōgan 13 (871), from Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade ({{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q1052981|lt=Sandai Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}); and in Gangyō 2 (878), from Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (Sandai Jitsuroku), reflecting successive promotions in {{ill|lt=divine rank|Q11591025|qid=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}{{sfn|Matsuura|1983|p=944}}. It is also listed as a minor Shikinaisha in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=477}}. For some time thereafter, the shrine did not appear in historical records, but perhaps because it was located in an isolated area away from settlements, its rites continued quietly without being supplanted by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kanjō|1=UNKNOWN}} deities{{sfn|Matsuura|1983|p=944}}. In the early modern period, references appear again: as “Sun Daimyōjin” in the 'Zōshin Inshūki' of Jōkyō 5 (1688), as “Sumi Daimyōjin” in the 'Ryōtō Jinja Shoaget-chō' of Hōreki 7 (1757), and as “Sun Shrine” in the late Edo-period 'Oki Kokiki-shū'{{sfn|Matsuura|1983|p=944}}. Originally, the shrine was enshrined near the coast at Sumi-no-ura due to its miraculous power, but as it came to be venerated as an {{ill|lt=ubusuna deity|Q1392097|qid=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}}, the site proved inconvenient for worship. Thus, in Ansei 2 (1855), it was relocated once to a place called Makome{{sfn|Kamada|1960|p=67}}{{sfn|Matsuura|1983|p=945}}. In Meiji 13 (1880), it was returned to the former site following a divine oracle, but again found inconvenient, leading to another relocation closer to the settlement{{sfn|Kamada|1960|p=68}}{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=311}}{{sfn|Matsuura|1983|p=943}}. At the initially proposed site of Tsukatani, a fatal accident occurred during construction for the relocation, so in Shōwa 3 (1928) the shrine was moved to a place called “Biya,” where it remains today{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=311}}. Because this relocation was approved under the pretext of establishing a village shrine, the former site could not be completely abolished{{sfn|Kamada|1960|p=68}}, and the subsidiary shrines remained there until they were moved to “Biya” in Shōwa 45 (1970){{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=312}}. === Legends === * It is said that the enshrined deity, Hinamadihime-no-Mikoto, drifted ashore and landed at a place called “Kantsuki (divine landing),” slightly south of the inlet of Sumi{{sfn|Kamada|1960|p=69}}. * Hinamadihime-no-Mikoto is said to be married to '''Uwaga-no-Mikoto''', the enshrined deity of {{ill|lt=Uuga-no-Mikoto Shrine|Q11449116|qid=Q11449116|1=Ujika Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Ama Town|Q685865|qid=Q685865|1=Ama, Shimane}}{{sfn|Kamada|1960|p=73}}. Furthermore, their child is said to be '''Nakirahime-no-Mikoto''', the enshrined deity of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nakirahime Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} in Ama Town{{sfn|Kamada|1960|p=73}}. Uwaga-no-Mikoto is said to have competed with the {{ill|lt=Oyama Shrine (Nishinoshima Town)|Q11434677|qid=Q11434677|lt=deity of Mount Ōyama|1=Ooyama Shrine}} over Hinamadihime-no-Mikoto. When she declared she would marry whoever won a contest of strength, they competed by throwing {{ill|lt=stones|Q14673|qid=Q14673|1=Pebble}} at each other from their respective islands{{sfn|Nagami|1968|p=628}}. At that time, a {{ill|lt=Amanojaku|Q2417460|qid=Q2417460|1=Amanojaku}} pulled at the sleeve of the Ōyama deity to interfere, resulting in Uwaga-no-Mikoto’s victory and Hinamadihime-no-Mikoto marrying him{{sfn|Nagami|1968|p=628}}. * A small golden snake that emerged from the white waves and came ashore at a place called “Mejika” is regarded as the shrine’s {{ill|lt=divine messenger|Q10444973|qid=Q10444973|1=Shinshi}}. It is said that this snake prevents impure persons from approaching the shrine{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=311}}. === Festivals === The festival is held on July 28{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=311}}{{efn|Before the adoption of the Gregorian calendar following the relocation in Shōwa 3 (1928), it was held on June 28 of the lunar calendar{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=311}}.}}. When the shrine was located at Sumi-no-ura, only the Yokoya (priest) and parish representatives participated; in calm seas they traveled by boat, and in rough weather they used mountain paths to visit the shrine{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=311}}. After the relocation to Biya, {{ill|lt=kagura|Q1348260|qid=Q1348260|1=Kagura}} performances came to be offered, and upon completion of the festival the parish representatives, district head, and priest held a naorai (ritual feast){{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|pp=311–312}}. General worshippers receive {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sacred sake|1=UNKNOWN}} at the shrine{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=312}}. The ujigami-kō (parish association ritual) is held on January 28{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=322}}. === Notes === === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist|20em}} === Bibliography === * {{Cite book |Japanese |author=Ōgi-no-Ya |chapter=545. Hinamochi-hime-no-Mikoto |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/121 |title=Gods of Shimane |publisher=Shimane Prefecture Shrine Association |id={{JP number|88012198}} |year=1987 |page=228 |ref=harv }} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Ōtsu Takehisa|chapter=Shinto-Buddhist rites|editor=Ishizuka Totoshi|title=Folklore of the Oki Islands: Emergency Survey Report on Oki Island Folklore|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Shrine Association|year=1973|pages=285–327|ref={{sfnref|Ōtsu|1973}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|editor=Onga District Board of Education|title=Onga District Gazetteer|year=1986|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9775495|publisher=Rinsen Shoten|series=Fukuoka Prefectural Local Gazetteer Series|ref={{sfnref|Onga District Board of Education, ed.|1986}}}} * {{cite journal|Japanese|author=Kamada Jun’ichi|year=1960|title=An Aspect of Shikinaisha in Oki Province|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ja/pid/3365296/1/35|journal=Kokugakuin Journal|volume=61|issue=2–3|pp=66–73|ref={{sfnref|Kamada|1960}}}} * {{Cite book |Japanese |editor1=Takahashi Tadashi |editor2=Mizushi Jūkichi |editor3=Itō Yukio |title=Divine Land Shimane |edition=Reprint |publisher=Shimane Prefecture Shrine Association |year=1996 |ref={{sfnref|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996}}}} * {{cite book |Japanese |author=Nagami Kazumasa |title=Kuroki Village Gazetteer |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ja/pid/3449471 |publisher=Kuroki Village Gazetteer Editorial Committee |year=1968 |ref={{sfnref|Nagami|1968}} }} * {{cite journal |Japanese |author1=Makabe Kunimoto |author2=Kuroda Ryūji |year=2016 |title=A Study on Shrine Main Halls of Oki-zukuri |journal=Architectural Institute of Japan Kinki Branch Research Reports, Planning Series|issue=56|pages=541–544|ref={{sfnref|Makabe & Kuroda|2016}}}} * {{Cite book |Japanese |author=Matsuura Yasumaro |chapter=4 Hinamadihime-no-Mikoto Shrine |editor=Shikinaisha Research Society |title=Shikinaisha Survey Report, Vol. 21, San’indō 4 |publisher=Kōgakkan University Press |year=1983 |pages=942–945 |id={{JP number|83032918}} |ref={{sfnref|Matsuura|1983}} }} * {{Cite book |Japanese |author=Matsuura Yasumaro |chapter=Gods Enshrined in Oki Province |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/25 |title=Gods of Shimane |publisher=Shimane Prefecture Shrine Association |id={{JP number|88012198}} |year=1987 |pages=36–53 |ref={{sfnref|Matsuura|1987}} }} * {{cite book|Japanese|editor=Mihara Johei|title=Topography of the Fukuoka District, Chikuzen Province|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9773297|year=1980|publisher=Bunken Shuppan|ref={{sfnref|Mihara, ed.|1980}}}} * {{Cite book |Japanese |author=Rika-no-Ya |chapter=557. Hinamadihime-no-Mikoto |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/123 |title=Gods of Shimane |publisher=Shimane Prefecture Shrine Association |id={{JP number|88012198}} |year=1987 |pages=232–233 |ref=harv }} === See also === * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province|Q11659043|qid=Q11659043|1=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province}} * {{ill|lt=Lighthouse|Q39715|qid=Q39715|1=Lighthouse}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Hinamajihimemikoto Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Hinamajihimemikoto Shrine]][[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:西ノ島町の歴史]] [[Category:Fire deities]]
